title: Costs and Cost Behaviour
description: Learn how different types of costs behave as business activity changes and why understanding cost behaviour is essential for budgeting, pricing and decision-making.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
items: Differentiate between fixed, variable and mixed costs.
Explain how costs behave as production volume changes.
Calculate total costs using fixed and variable costs.
Understand why cost behaviour is important for managerial decisions.
Recognize common mistakes when analyzing costs.

sections.title: Introduction
content: Every business incurs costs in order to produce goods or provide services. Understanding how these costs behave is one of the most important foundations of business economics.
Managers use cost behaviour to estimate future expenses, prepare budgets, determine selling prices and evaluate profitability.
Not all costs change in the same way. Some remain constant regardless of production, while others increase as more products are produced.

sections.title: What Is a Cost?
content: A cost is the value of resources consumed in producing goods or services.
Costs may include wages, raw materials, rent, electricity, insurance, maintenance and many other expenses required to operate a business.
Businesses must carefully manage costs to remain profitable while continuing to deliver value to customers.

sections.title: Fixed Costs
content: Fixed costs remain constant within a relevant range of activity, regardless of how many units are produced.
Whether a factory produces 100 units or 10,000 units, its monthly rent usually remains the same.
Examples of fixed costs include rent, insurance premiums, salaries of administrative staff and property taxes.

sections.title: Example: Factory Rent
content: A factory pays $20,000 per month in rent.
If the factory produces 500 products, rent is still $20,000.
If production increases to 2,000 products, rent remains $20,000, assuming the factory does not need additional facilities.

sections.title: Variable Costs
content: Variable costs change directly with the level of production or sales.
As production increases, total variable costs increase proportionally. If production decreases, variable costs also decrease.
Examples include raw materials, packaging, sales commissions and fuel used during production.

sections.title: Example: Raw Materials
content: A company spends $8 on raw materials for every product it manufactures.
Producing 100 units results in variable costs of $800.
Producing 500 units results in variable costs of $4,000.

sections.title: Mixed (Semi-Variable) Costs
content: Some costs contain both fixed and variable components. These are known as mixed or semi-variable costs.
For example, a mobile phone contract may include a fixed monthly fee plus additional charges based on usage.
Many utility costs, maintenance contracts and transportation expenses behave this way.

sections.title: Example: Electricity
content: A factory pays a fixed monthly connection fee of $500.
In addition, it pays for every kilowatt-hour of electricity consumed during production.
The total electricity bill therefore contains both fixed and variable elements.

sections.title: Relevant Range
content: Cost behaviour assumptions are only valid within a certain range of activity called the relevant range.
If production increases significantly, businesses may need to rent another factory, hire additional managers or purchase more equipment.
When this happens, costs that were previously fixed may increase in steps.

sections.title: Total Cost
content: Total cost is the sum of fixed costs and total variable costs.
A simple formula is:
Total Cost = Fixed Costs + (Variable Cost per Unit × Number of Units Produced)
This formula is widely used for budgeting and profitability analysis.

sections.title: Worked Example
content: A company has fixed costs of $40,000 per month.
Variable cost is $12 per unit.
If 3,000 units are produced:
Total Variable Cost = 3,000 × $12 = $36,000

sections.title: Why Cost Behaviour Matters
content: Understanding cost behaviour helps managers estimate future costs and predict how profits change when production changes.
It also supports pricing decisions, budgeting, investment analysis and break-even calculations.
Without understanding cost behaviour, businesses may make poor decisions based on incorrect assumptions about future expenses.

sections.title: Example: Shipping Company
content: A shipping company operates a fleet of vessels.
Insurance, office salaries and software subscriptions are largely fixed costs.
Fuel consumption, port charges and certain maintenance expenses increase with vessel operations and are more variable in nature.
Understanding which costs are fixed and which are variable helps managers estimate operating costs for different voyage schedules.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: Assuming every cost is either completely fixed or completely variable.
Ignoring the relevant range when estimating future costs.
Confusing total costs with cost per unit.
Believing that fixed costs never change under any circumstances.

sections.title: Expert Insight
content: One of the first questions experienced managers ask when evaluating a business decision is: 'Which costs will actually change?' Correctly identifying cost behaviour allows managers to forecast profitability, evaluate investments and respond effectively to changing market conditions.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
items: Costs represent the resources consumed to operate a business.
Fixed costs remain constant within the relevant range of activity.
Variable costs change directly with production or sales volume.
Mixed costs contain both fixed and variable components.
Total Cost = Fixed Costs + Total Variable Costs.
Understanding cost behaviour is essential for budgeting, pricing and managerial decision-making.

sections.title: Reflection
questions: Why is it important to distinguish between fixed and variable costs when planning production?
Can a cost that is fixed today become variable in the future? Explain your reasoning.
How might understanding cost behaviour improve decision-making in industries such as shipping, manufacturing or retail?
